title: Oil-on-water marbling
subtitle: Floating oil colours on plain water to produce irregular stone-like patterns
excerpt: Oil-on-water marbling predates carrageenan-bath marbling by several centuries. The method is simpler: oil colours thinned with turpentine are dropped onto plain water, where they float and spread. The paper is laid on the surface and lifted, bringing away a print. The results are less predictable than carrageenan work but have a characteristic veined, mineral quality.
sourceNotes: C. W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1853, revised 1881). Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (English translation 1894). Both public domain. Oil-on-water marbling is described by Woolnough as an earlier technique than the Turkish carrageenan-bath method.

glossaryTerms.definition: A marbling technique in which oil-based colours thinned with turpentine or white spirit are floated on the surface of plain water.
A marbling pattern that resembles the veining and patterning of semiprecious stone, typically produced in oil-on-water or early carrageenan techniques.

content.text: Oil-on-water marbling produces the characteristic
stone pattern
: irregular, biomorphic shapes without the clean combed regularity of a carrageenan-bath marble. The
oil marbling

technique needs no preparation of the water surface. This makes it the faster of the two systems to set up. The trade-off is less control and more variation from print to print. Many marblers prefer it for that reason.

Work in a ventilated room with the window open when using turpentine or white spirit. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray. Dispose of solvent-soaked rags in a sealed tin, not a heaped bin liner: solvent rags can self-heat.
What you need
Tray: a flat tray about 30 mm deep, larger than the paper. Fill with plain cold water to a depth of about 20 mm.

Colours: oil-based artist colours (artist-quality oil paint or liquid acrylic made oil-compatible) thinned with odourless mineral spirit or turpentine to a very thin, flowing consistency. Test by dropping one drop onto the water; it should spread in a large circle. If it sinks, the colour is too thick. If it spreads until almost invisible, it is too thin.
Paper: alum-mordanted paper. Brush alum solution (potash alum, 1 tablespoon per 500 ml water) onto the sheet and allow to dry fully before marbling.
Floating the colours
Drop the first colour onto the water surface from an eyedropper or the tip of a brush. The drop should spread in a circle.
Drop a second colour into the centre of the first circle. It will push the first colour outward.
Add three to five colours in total, building up a surface of nested circles.
Optionally, draw the marbling stylus across the surface to create veined effects.
Printing the sheet
Hold the mordanted sheet at the two short ends. Lower the centre onto the surface first, then release the ends so the sheet lies flat on the water.
After 15 seconds, peel the sheet off in a single smooth motion from one short end.
Rinse the print briefly under cold running water to remove any loose oil colour. Hang to dry.
Care

Store oil-marbled paper flat in acid-free folders. The oil colour may take several days to fully cure on the paper surface; keep sheets separated until fully dry to prevent colour transferring between sheets.
